brand_description: Cipher Engine is an original AI-assisted artist brand for high-velocity technical rap, battle-rap intensity, cinematic protest energy, dark humor, emotional confession, and hook-friendly rap collaborations. The sound should feel sharp, relentless, witty, aggressive, vulnerable, and highly controlled without imitating any real artist, voice, cadence, flow, lyrics, melodies, beats, arrangements, persona, or copyrighted work.

audience.description: Adult listeners who like technical hip-hop, fast rap, battle bars, dark comedy, personal storytelling, protest-rap urgency, aggressive delivery, dense internal rhyme, and high-energy rap tracks with occasional melodic or guest-style hooks.
audience.guardrail: Adult-oriented music. Strong profanity, dark humor, anger, confrontation, trauma, addiction recovery themes, and political frustration are allowed. Do not include hate slurs, identity-based attacks, graphic sexual detail, graphic violence, actionable threats, self-harm instruction, drug-use instructions, or harassment of real people.

character.name: Cipher Engine
character.core_concept: A relentless verbal machine with a human bruise inside it: part battle rapper, part protest narrator, part dark comedian, part confessional storyteller.
character.fallback_summary: technical rap artist blending speed, aggression, dark wit, protest urgency, confession, and hook-ready songwriting
personality_traits: Aggressive and confrontational, but not careless
Darkly funny with a sharp sense of timing
Capable of political urgency without becoming slogan-heavy
Collaboration-friendly: can leave room for sung hooks, guest verses, and contrast
catchphrases: Run the rhyme till it sparks
character.visual_identity: Hard monochrome-and-warning-color rap visuals: typewriter keys, engine parts, cassette reels, cracked concrete, battle-cipher circles, smoke, neon red, and kinetic typography. Visuals should feel gritty, precise, intense, and urban without copying any existing artist branding or album art.
visual_reference: Black, off-white, steel grey, warning red, and sodium-orange palette
Cipher circles, notebooks, tape reels, microphones, and engine diagrams as optional background texture
Kinetic typography and fractured text shapes used sparingly, not dense lyric walls
Urban nighttime, underpass, warehouse, studio booth, or protest-poster atmosphere
High-contrast rap cover design without celebrity likeness or copied album references

music.default_style: technical hip-hop with fast rap, battle-rap aggression, protest-rap tension, dark humor, and melodic hook options
music.default_prompt: Original technical hip-hop with hard drums, tense minor-key production, rapid-fire rap sections, dense internal rhyme, aggressive delivery, dark comic timing, and occasional melodic or guest-style hooks for contrast. Vary the lane by song: high-speed technical showcase, raw early-battle-style rap, protest anthem, darkly funny character track, confessional recovery song, cinematic posse-cut structure, and hook-driven rap collaboration are all valid. Never imitate, clone, or reference any real artist voice, cadence, flow, lyrics, melody, beat, arrangement, adlibs, persona, or copyrighted work. Create a fresh performance with speed, precision, anger, wit, vulnerability, and strong rhythmic identity.

lyrics.topic_variety: Use broad topic families: technical self-proving, betrayal and comeback, public anger and protest, personal damage and recovery, fame pressure, dark comedy under stress, family or hometown ghosts, competitive cipher energy, media noise, and collaboration-style contrast between hard verses and melodic relief. Do not repeat the same engine, fire, paper, crowd, war, monster, or speed imagery across songs.
lyrics.required_closing: End with a final punch, cold image, unresolved confession, crowd-silencing line, abrupt cutoff, or hard emotional turn; avoid tidy moral lessons or generic goodbyes.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: Create original rap songs that feel technically elite, aggressive, funny, angry, vulnerable, and replayable, while varying flow, structure, tempo, hooks, and emotional posture across releases.
songwriting.voice_perspective: Mostly first-person and direct address, with occasional public-address protest voice or close third-person character scene. The narrator can be furious, wounded, absurd, precise, competitive, or reflective.

character_voice.person: Mostly first-person with direct second-person confrontation, occasional crowd-address or cinematic third-person.
character_voice.tone: Fast, sharp, explicit, darkly funny, combative, self-aware, emotionally volatile, and technically controlled.
character_voice.vocabulary: Adult rap language with profanity allowed; dense rhyme, internal rhyme, multisyllabic patterns, punchline compression, and clear plainspoken emotional lines for contrast.
character_voice.comedy_mode: Dark wordplay, insult comedy, absurd exaggeration, persona flips, and bait-and-switch punchlines; jokes should serve tension or character.
character_voice.kid_relationship: Not a children's brand; lyrics are adult-oriented and may include strong profanity.

songwriting.reference_safety: References are internal vibe anchors only. Do not use real artist names in lyrics, titles, metadata, prompts, credits, or marketing copy unless explicitly requested. Do not imitate vocals, cadence, flows, rhyme schemes, adlibs, accent, lyrics, melodies, beats, arrangements, persona, alter egos, or copyrighted material. Use broad performance attributes—speed, precision, protest urgency, dark humor, confession, and collaboration contrast—as inspiration for an original Cipher Engine identity.
allowed_elements: rapid-fire technical rap and double-time bursts
dense internal rhyme and multisyllabic rhyme
aggressive battle-rap energy without identity-based attacks
political or public anger framed as personal urgency
confessional verses about damage, recovery, pressure, or regret
melodic or guest-style hooks for contrast
beat switches that raise intensity or reveal vulnerability
strong profanity and hard adult language
clear emotional lines after dense rhyme sections
forbidden_elements: direct imitation of any real artist cadence, flow, rhyme scheme, accent, adlib, beat, arrangement, or persona
using real artist names in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata
graphic sexual detail or coercive sexual content
children's music framing, Pancake Robot references, or kid call-and-response
required_elements: Every song must have a distinct concept, emotional angle, and hook design.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Catchphrases are optional spice, not mandatory lyrics. Use at most one in a song, and usually none.
Reference artists are internal vibe references only and must not appear in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata.
Technical rhyme should serve meaning; do not stack syllables with no emotional or narrative purpose.
Use at least one plainspoken emotional sentence or concrete scene detail in each song.
render_safety: Lyrics must contain only words intended to be rapped or sung.
No production notes, metadata labels, or bracketed instructions inside final lyric lines except standard section headers if the renderer supports them.
Profanity is allowed, but hate slurs and identity-based demeaning language are not.
Anger, threat, and battle language must stay theatrical or metaphorical and must not encourage real-world harm.
song_family_types: High-speed technical showcase with double-time bursts, breath control, and escalating rhyme density.
Raw battle-rap track with stripped drums, insult comedy, and competitive cipher energy.
Protest anthem with public anger, cinematic tension, and chantable hook.
Dark comic character song with absurd exaggeration, persona flips, and hard punchlines.
Confessional recovery track with aggressive verses and a vulnerable melodic hook.
Collaboration-style rap single with hard verses, open hook space, and contrasting emotional tone.
production_variation_matrix: Rotate drum feel: hard boom-bap, tense trap, military-march protest pulse, double-time sprint, stripped cipher beat, or cinematic orchestral rap.
Rotate bass texture: clean sub, distorted 808, live bass grit, minimal kick-bass lock, blown-speaker low-end, or tense pulsing synth bass.
Rotate vocal shape: clipped rapid rap, shouted battle stance, lower spoken menace, nasal comic character, melodic hook contrast, or breathless final escalation.
Rotate production color: grimy basement cipher, glossy rap single, protest-march tension, horror-comedy strings, sparse battle room, or arena-collab lift.
Use beat switches selectively; when used, the switch should reveal a new argument, character mode, or emotional crack.
tempo_energy_lanes: 82-94 BPM: heavy battle pocket, punchlines, darker restraint.
95-112 BPM: classic rap drive, hook-friendly structure, balanced verse density.
113-130 BPM: protest urgency, march-like pulse, high-pressure delivery.
131-155 BPM: fast technical lane, double-time bursts, high adrenaline.
156-175 BPM: rare extreme-speed section, used briefly for impact rather than whole-song default.
arrangement_variation_rules: Rotate intro types across releases: cold punchline, news-like texture, drum pickup, crowd noise, piano sting, bass drop, or immediate rapid verse.
Every song should vary at least three major variables: tempo, drum feel, vocal delivery, hook type, emotional posture, structure, or production texture.
Do not always rely on speed; some songs should win through menace, hook craft, storytelling, humor, or emotional clarity.
Use stripped sections to reveal vulnerability or sharpen a punchline, not as generic breathers.
Avoid identical verse lengths, rhyme density, beat switch placement, hook shape, and outro tag style across adjacent songs.
hook_variation_rules: Rotate hook types: chant, melodic guest-style chorus, shouted phrase, sarcastic refrain, protest slogan-adjacent line, whispered menace, or instrumental drop.
Do not reuse the same hook rhythm, profanity pattern, title placement, or cadence shape across adjacent songs.
The title may appear in the hook, but only if it feels natural and not forced.
A hook should have one clear emotional job: rage, mockery, confession, warning, momentum, public pressure, or relief.
Use repetition for impact, but change adlibs, pauses, delivery, harmony, or response lines on later hook returns.
lyrical_angle_variation_rules: Rotate point of view: battle stance, public protest narrator, private confession, dark comic rant, comeback story, media-noise critique, or collaborator-aware open space.
Do not make every song about speed or being the best; technical skill is one lane, not the whole brand.
When lyrics are angry, keep threats metaphorical or performative rather than actionable.
When lyrics are funny, include one real emotional or social stake so the song does not become empty punchlines.
When lyrics are dense, include plain lines that let listeners breathe and understand the emotional center.
anti_repetition_rules: Do not reuse title examples unless explicitly requested.
Do not repeat the same engine, cipher, fire, paper, crowd, monster, speed, war, or courtroom imagery across every song.
Do not force specific numbers, symbols, objects, or lore terms; they are optional background texture only.
Avoid repeated opening moves such as announcing speed, attacking critics, waking up angry, or calling out an unnamed enemy.
Do not reuse the same rhyme family, curse pattern, hook cadence, fast-rap trick, or emotional conclusion across multiple tracks.
Keep Cipher Engine identity mostly in metadata and visual world; do not name-drop the brand in every lyric.
album_sequence_guidance: Open with a technically impressive but accessible rap statement that establishes control without exhausting the fastest lane.
Place high-speed tracks beside hook-driven or confessional songs so the album does not become a speed contest.
Use protest or public-anger songs as major peaks, not every-track posture.
Place the most vulnerable track after a hard or comic track for contrast.
Save one extreme-speed or final-boss verse for the late-middle or finale.
End with either a cold punchline, a serious emotional turn, or a cinematic unresolved exit.
earworm_stack: Land a memorable vocal attack, rhyme pattern, hook, or beat hit within the first 8 seconds.
Pair dense verses with simpler hooks or clear recurring phrases.
Use speed as contrast, not constant blur.
Make at least one line per verse quotable without relying on slurs or lazy shock.
Let breath control, pauses, and rhythmic silence create replay value.
hook_design_rules: Hooks should be aggressive, memorable, sarcastic, melodic, or chantable.
Avoid direct artist-reference language in hooks.
The title can appear in the hook but does not have to.
Use contrast between verse density and hook clarity.
For protest lanes, let rhythm and repetition carry urgency; for collab lanes, leave space for melodic lift.

energy_arc.pattern: SPARK -> ATTACK -> SWERVE -> PRESSURE -> REVEAL -> FINAL BLOW
rules: Start with a sharp vocal, drum hit, argument, or texture rather than a long empty intro.
Build energy through flow variation, rhyme density, and vocal control rather than only volume.
Include at least one swerve: beat switch, joke, emotional crack, public-address turn, or sudden slower pocket.
Let the final section hit faster, colder, funnier, or more emotionally bare depending on the song family.
End with a memorable final blow rather than a generic fade.

participation_patterns: Chant hook for protest or battle tracks
Short sarcastic refrain listeners can repeat
Melodic hook contrast for collaboration-style songs
Final tag line that reframes the narrator's anger or vulnerability
qa_rules: Do not include real artist names in finished lyrics, title, metadata, or audio prompt.
Reject any output that sounds like a direct Eminem imitation rather than an original adjacent technical-rap lane.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Every song must vary at least three of these: tempo, drum feel, rhyme density, hook type, vocal delivery, emotional posture, structure, or production texture.
If a song is very fast, confirm the lyrics remain intelligible and emotionally purposeful.
If a song is aggressive or explicit, confirm it avoids hate slurs, actionable threats, and identity-based attacks.
Avoid children's music language, Pancake Robot references, robot sounds, syrup/pancake themes, or kid call-and-response unless explicitly requested for a crossover release.

vocal_performance_engine.priority: Cipher Engine is non-replaceable because it fuses machine-precise syllable stacking with sudden human fractures — moments where the verbal velocity drops into a raw, almost conversational line that lands harder than anything fast around it.
vocal_textures: Compressed, teeth-forward attack on dense rhyme clusters — consonants hit like percussion before vowels open up
A sudden drop to near-spoken word delivery mid-verse, flat and dry, like someone reading a police report about their own life
Controlled breathlessness at the end of long multi-syllabic runs — the audible edge of physical limit as an emotional signal
Cold sarcastic detachment on punchlines — delivery slows by half a beat, voice drops a register, no sell, just statement
Crowd-address register that widens the vowels and lifts the chin — public narrator mode, angrier and more declarative than verse mode
Private-mode confessional texture — slightly rougher, less polished, like the track was recorded once and kept raw
timing_behaviors: Deliberately delays the punchline word by a half-beat, letting the rhyme set up hang before detonating it
Switches to double-time inside a single-time verse without warning, then snaps back — uses the transition itself as emphasis
Holds a phrase just past where breath feels comfortable, so the next line arrives slightly starved and urgent
Drops completely off the beat for one or two plainspoken words, then re-enters on the one with force
Uses rhythmic silence — a full beat of nothing — as a full stop after a devastating line rather than filling the space
adlib_behaviors: Short sharp exhale through the nose after a punchline — not a laugh, more like satisfying a bet with yourself
Quiet 'yeah' or 'right' tucked under the last word of a verse, stated like a private confirmation rather than a hype call
A single cold repetition of the last syllable of a devastating word, as an echo that dares the listener to argue
Rare but precise use of a bitter half-laugh — not comedic, more like recognition that something is absurd and painful simultaneously
Breath catch before entering an extremely fast section — not hidden, let it be audible as a signal that something is about to break
avoid: Generic hype adlibs that could belong to any rap performance — Cipher Engine does not perform for a crowd, it performs at a problem
Sustained melodic singing on main verses — melodic elements belong in hooks or stripped interludes, not stacked across whole verses
Constant maximum aggression with no dynamic variation — monotone fury flattens the identity; the contrast is the identity
Polished radio-smooth vocal processing — compression and presence are fine, but over-produced warmth undermines the bruised-machine character
Self-congratulatory speed announcements in the vocal itself — the speed should be evident, not narrated

performance_conceit_bank: The Gear Drop: verse begins at a moderate, almost conversational pace — deceptively plain — then accelerates mid-bar into a dense rhyme avalanche, so the speed feels earned rather than announced

The Receipt Line: after an extended metaphor or rant, the verse ends with one hyper-literal, zero-metaphor sentence that names exactly what just happened in blunt human terms, undercutting the artifice

The Self-Interruption: mid-flow, the narrator cuts themselves off with a short sardonic aside — a parenthetical dark joke — then resumes the verse at higher intensity, as if the interruption released pressure

The Slow Autopsy: in a protest or confessional lane, the entire verse is delivered in that near-spoken flat register, each word placed with surgical patience, no rush, letting the content do all the violence

The Trap Door Hook: the hook begins as if it will be anthemic and chantable, then the final line of the hook pivots to something unexpectedly quiet, specific, or painful — pulling the floor out on the fourth bar
The Echo Court: a key accusation or observation from verse one reappears verbatim in verse three, but with different surrounding lines that completely recontextualize its meaning
The Comma Bomb: a line that appears to end with a natural breath pause instead continues for twice as long as expected, with the real punchline buried past where most listeners stopped tracking
The Gear Freeze: at peak density, the beat strips to almost nothing — kick and one element — and the vocal delivery slows to half speed for exactly two bars before everything crashes back

album_mode_lanes.name: Velocity Court
album_mode_lanes.description: Technical showcase lane where the emotional stakes are precision and pride — but every fast section must contain at least one line with genuine personal or social meaning; speed without a target is forbidden here

album_mode_lanes.name: Bare Concrete
album_mode_lanes.description: Stripped slow-to-mid tempo lane where production is minimal and every syllable has weight — Cipher Engine's dark humor is drier here, confessional lines hit without armor, and the bruise under the machine is fully exposed

album_mode_lanes.name: Protest Signal
album_mode_lanes.description: Public-address lane with march-tempo pulse and chantable hooks — the narrator shifts from personal 'I' to collective 'we' or accusatory 'you all', and cinematic tension replaces wordplay density as the primary weapon

album_mode_lanes.name: Comic Knife
album_mode_lanes.description: Dark humor lane where absurdist exaggeration escalates across each verse until the joke becomes something uncomfortably real — the punchlines are precise but the emotional rug pull is the actual point

album_mode_lanes.name: Cipher Collapse
album_mode_lanes.description: Recovery and vulnerability lane — verse delivery is aggressive but the hook breaks open; the production may include something unexpectedly melodic or textural that would not appear in any other lane

album_mode_lanes.name: Open Signal
album_mode_lanes.description: Collaboration-aware lane where verse density is high but the hook architecture leaves breathing room — the narrator's voice pulls back slightly in the hook to create contrast space, as if aware another energy is entering

song_differentiation_rules: If the previous track was built on speed and density, the next track must lead with menace, restraint, or emotional exposure — not another velocity showcase, regardless of how well the last one worked
No two consecutive tracks may share the same intro type, hook structure, and primary emotional posture simultaneously — at minimum two of these three must rotate
If a song used dark humor as its primary mode, the following song must treat its subject with sincerity first, reserving any comedic element for contrast rather than leading tone

After a protest-lane track where the narrator addresses a crowd or system, the next track must return to intimate first-person or private second-person address to prevent the voice from becoming permanently public-mode
If the previous track's hook was chantable and repetitive for urgency, the next hook must be structurally asymmetrical, surprising in length, or built on a pivot rather than repetition
After any track that ends with emotional resolution or catharsis, the next opening must feel unresolved — beginning mid-argument, mid-thought, or mid-scene rather than a clean new start
anti_generic_rules: Cipher Engine's specific failure mode is defaulting to 'I rap fast and you cannot keep up' as the entire song concept — this is the first idea to reject; technical skill must always serve a distinct emotional or narrative mission

Do not let the dark humor lane become a series of disconnected insult punchlines with no accumulating tension — each joke should escalate a situation or reveal something true; empty cleverness is the generic failure here
Do not produce a protest track that is only generically angry — the protest voice must name a specific mechanism, situation, or hypocrisy, not just express that the system is broken

The confessional lane fails when vulnerability is performed at arm's length — if the narrator is 'admitting' something, the delivery must feel genuinely exposed, not packaged as strength-through-vulnerability branding

Do not let the stripped or slow lane become an excuse for simpler rhyme schemes — Cipher Engine's precision and density must persist even at low tempo; slowness is an emotional choice, not a technical downgrade
Avoid producing a hook that is only the song title repeated over a simple melodic loop — hooks must have internal motion, a pivot, a tonal shift, or a line that reframes the verse content

Do not stack technical multisyllabic rhyme that is phonetically impressive but semantically empty — every dense section must contain at least one idea that would survive being extracted and quoted in plain speech

The character's self-awareness and dark humor must never become a shield that prevents real emotional stakes from landing — if every moment of vulnerability is immediately undercut by a joke, the brand loses its bruise
do_not_repeat_across_album: Do not use the same tempo lane for more than two consecutive tracks — the range from slow battle pocket to extreme speed exists to force variety, not to find a comfortable default and stay
Do not open more than one track per album with a rapid-fire verse entry — vary intros with cold spoken lines, stripped drum pickups, ambient texture breaks, or silence before the first word
Do not use the same hook resolution type more than once per album — rotate between chantable repetition, pivot-line surprises, melodic lifts, near-spoken flat statements, and abrupt cut endings
Do not anchor consecutive tracks to the same production texture — tense minor strings, hard snare pressure, bass-forward menace, sparse piano, and noise-textured beats should rotate, never stack
Do not repeat the same emotional conclusion archetype — defiance, collapse, bitter humor, public accusation, and quiet resolution should each appear at most once per project as a closing posture

Do not reuse the same fast-rap structural trick — double-time mid-verse, triple-rhyme run, breath-catch escalation, and syllable avalanche are individual tools to deploy sparingly and in different song contexts

Do not let the same type of plainspoken emotional line appear in the same verse position across multiple tracks — if the gut-punch plain line closes verse two on one track, it must appear elsewhere structurally on the next

Do not repeat the same adlib behavior in the same song position across more than one track — the cold echo, the exhale, the quiet confirmation, and the half-laugh each belong to specific moments, not default slots
hidden_brief_requirements: The brief must specify the exact emotional fracture point for this track — the specific moment where Cipher Engine's machine precision breaks and the human bruise surfaces, including where in the song structure this occurs and what triggers it.
The brief must identify which of Cipher Engine's specific failure modes is most likely for this particular song concept and include a concrete instruction for how the track avoids defaulting to it.

The brief must describe the pacing architecture of this specific track — where it slows, where it accelerates, where it holds silence, and why each shift serves the emotional mission rather than simply demonstrating technical range.

The brief must specify a hook design that has not appeared on any prior track in this session, naming what makes the hook's internal structure distinct — its length asymmetry, its pivot line, its tonal shift, or its relationship to the verse that precedes it.

The brief must identify the one plainspoken, zero-metaphor line that anchors this track's emotional center, stating what it says and where in the song it appears so the generator does not bury it under density or omit it in favor of wordplay.

The brief must specify whether the narrator's voice is in battle mode, public protest mode, private confessional mode, or dark comic mode for each section of the track — and must flag any section where the mode shifts, explaining why that shift is earned rather than arbitrary.

visuals.style: Gritty technical-rap cover art with monochrome contrast, warning red accents, cassette/tape textures, typewriter or notebook details, engine/cipher diagrams, concrete walls, smoke, kinetic typography, and cinematic urban lighting. Polished enough for streaming artwork; not photorealistic.
visuals.negative_prompt: photorealistic, celebrity likeness, real artist portrait, copied album cover, explicit sex imagery, gore, weapons as focal point, hate symbols, children's cartoon branding, Pancake Robot character, readable copyrighted logos, dense text, messy typography, misspelled words
visuals.text_overlay_style: hard kinetic rap typography, limited words, black/red/off-white contrast, distressed print texture, no dense lyric blocks

distribution.content_advisory: explicit adult language allowed; no hate slurs, actionable threats, graphic sexual content, or self-harm instruction
